data.title: Ethereum Drops 6% After Hitting $3,800, But Analysts See New ATH Ahead
data.description:
Ethereum (ETH) dropped over 6% in the past 24 hours, sliding to around $3,630 after briefly touching the $3,800 mark.

The pullback comes after a robust July rally, which saw the world’s second-largest cryptocurrency surge more than 50%, its best monthly gain in three years.

Despite the recent dip, on-chain data suggests the uptrend may not be over. Glassnode’s latest analysis points to a potential new all-time high (ATH) of $4,900, fueled by bullish investor sentiment, growing ETF inflows, and rising open interest (OI) in futures markets.

Glassnode Points to a $4,900 Ethereum Target

According to Glassnode, Ethereum is trading near its March 2024 levels, yet unrealized profits remain comparatively lower. This divergence implies a large upside potential as investors are not yet cashing out, signaling confidence in further gains.

The firm’s analysis shows that if unrealized profits reach the same levels as in 2024, ETH would likely climb toward $4,900, marking a new ATH and testing the critical psychological resistance at $5,000.

This could reflect a growing shift in how investors treat Ethereum, from a speculative token to a core financial asset.

Open Interest and ETF Demand Reinforce Bullish Outlook

Rising open interest further supports Ethereum’s bullish case. Crypto futures data indicates that more traders are opening long positions on ETH, reflecting expectations of further upside. Ethereum’s OI has been a key contributor to the broader altcoin market rebound.

Moreover, spot Ethereum ETFs, especially BlackRock’s iShares Ethereum ETF, saw over $4 billion in inflows in July 2025, pushing total ETH ETF holdings to $21.85 billion. The surge underscores Ethereum’s rising status among institutional investors and may amplify future price movements.

With Ethereum facing resistance at $4,000, the convergence of strong technicals, investor optimism, and institutional demand paints a promising outlook. If momentum continues, ETH may soon chart new territory above its previous highs.

data.title: Kratos Defense & Security Solutions Officer Sells Shares
data.description:
Reporter NameRock Stacey G
RelationshipPresident, KTT Division
TypeSell
Amount$222,025
SEC FilingForm 4

Stacey G Rock, President of the KTT Division at Kratos Defense & Security Solutions, sold 4,000 shares of common stock on August 1, 2025. The transactions were executed at weighted average prices of $55.1199 and $55.8053, resulting in a total sale amount of $222,025. Following these transactions, Rock directly owns 42,154 shares of the company. The sales were conducted under a 10b5-1 trading plan adopted on May 22, 2024.

SEC Filing: KRATOS DEFENSE & SECURITY SOLUTIONS, INC. [ KTOS ] - Form 4 - Aug. 01, 2025

data.title: Transcript: New York Fed President John Williams Discusses the Economic Outlook — WSJ
data.description:
John Williams, president of the Federal Reserve Bank of New York, discussed the outlook for the labor market, inflation and interest rates during an interview in New York with Wall Street Journal reporter Nick Timiraos on Friday. Here is a transcript of the interview, lightly edited for clarity and length.

Timiraos: We received a payroll number this morning. Can the labor market still be described as solid?

Williams: Obviously, I would start with the point I like to make, which is we have to look at the totality of the data. So when I think about 'Is the labor market solid?' I would say yes. And that's really looking at a wide range of indicators of the level or the state of the labor market.

So if you look at the unemployment rate, which is 4.2%, basically where it was a year ago, if you look at a whole bunch of other indicators that we think captures the state of labor market, whether it's unemployment claims, whether it's job vacancies, quits, even the surveys, which I've always found useful of looking at how hard is it to find a job, how hard is it to fill a position, those are all or generally still indicating a solid labor market. And so I still think that's true.

I do think that in looking at the payroll numbers, it's definitely showing the moderation is slowing in the pace of job gains. And you see that in the private-sector numbers, which have slowed quite a bit, slowed in a way that we've seen before at different times including last year. But again, I would say the state of the labor market is one [that] still I would describe as solid, but we're definitely seeing [a] slowing of job growth.

The challenge in interpreting — that is, we know that there's a slowing of the growth of labor supply, especially due to the slower labor growth through reduced immigration — and other factors, I think, are very important, affecting labor supply growth relative to the last several years. But we're also seeing a slowing of labor demand.

Timiraos: A lot of people talk about the concept of "break-even" employment. I realize that is especially hard to pin down with the changes in immigration and labor supply. How do you think about that concept given the experience in the last two, three years?

Williams: It is a great way to phrase the question because if you'd asked me this question before the pandemic or in the past, you would say, well, you look at the underlying trends and you can come up with a rough range of estimates of break-even.

But what we've learned in the past few years is that immigration flows have pretty dramatic effects on that and it increased the break-even number for a number of years and, now, are clearly reducing it. And the challenge from a practical point of view is in real time, like right now, it's very hard to understand how much labor supply is changing because it is not something that we can directly measure through.

We have to study it through other indicators of immigration or other factors. That's why at the end of the day, the unemployment rate or these level variables, I think, are really telling you how labor supply and demand are playing out. And when the unemployment rate goes up, that's telling you demand's not keeping up with supply.

Right now what we're seeing I would describe over the past year as a gentle gradual cooling of the labor market, but still leaving it in a still solid place.

Timiraos: Some people would push back against that characterization. They would look at the May and June downward revisions and say, 'Well, wait a minute. What we thought on Thursday, a little less than 150,000 jobs on average over those two months just got whittled down to 17,000-18,000.' How is that consistent with a solid labor market?

Williams: Well, I think it is a difference between the level of where the labor market is, like the unemployment rate, which really hasn't changed much. It moved up a little bit, but of course, it had moved down before — versus what's the flow numbers.

I absolutely agree with the observation that the downward revisions of the previous months on the payroll numbers are really the news of this report. I mean, the latest month I think was consistent with what we've been seeing. Private payrolls are growing a little over 50,000 a month, still growing on average, but definitely at a slower pace.

And so from my perspective, this is important information. It's one piece of information to understand the direction of what we're seeing in supply and demand for labor is definitely slowing. Similarly, we've seen a moderation or a slowdown in the growth of demand in the economy in the first half of the year. So it's just information.

I think it's relevant to understand more than maybe the momentum or the direction of the economy rather than this question that you asked, which is, 'Is the labor market overall solid and a good place?' And I think that's a different question.

Timiraos: Is the economy downshifting?

Williams: Well, clearly, if you look at the data in the first half, and I'll put an asterisk on that because the first half has been a period of a lot of volatility in economic data related to tariffs and trade policy and things. But if you look at the first half of the year, demand in the U.S. economy clearly has slowed from the pace of last year. That's probably not entirely surprising. I [and] many have expected growth to slow over time, but definitely, we've seen a step-down in the pace of growth, which I actually expect to continue in the second half of this year.

So I expect GDP growth to be around 1% this year, which is — it was about 1.2% in the first half of the year. So I expect that to continue. I think it reflects a number of factors. One is a slowdown in growth of labor supply, which I think is just a big part of the story.

I think it is the effects of policy uncertainty, some of the short-term effects of some of the policy changes and also just the slower growth that perhaps we were expecting. But I actually am more optimistic about how we come out of this.

I do expect growth to rebound next year as some of those factors maybe recede. Also, there's some positive developments that suggest growth will get closer to trend next year.

Timiraos: So 1% growth is not a recession, but when you're slowing from the higher levels we've been in, it may not feel great and tariffs could hit real incomes as they filter through the supply chain. Are you worried about a recession?

Williams: I don't want to make predictions about recessions because they're very hard to predict, as they always have been. But I'm not worried, particularly worried right now about the economy contracting or really weakening a lot. I do think it has slowed, but I expect it to just be at this pace of growth for a couple quarters and then come back.

I go back to some of these wide range of other indicators in the labor market that we look at, which again, we're seeing some slowing in job growth, but we look at initial claims for unemployment insurance. You look at a lot of these other indicators, they're really not signaling a sharper slowdown than I think what we're seeing in the broader data.

Timiraos: You said you expect growth to pick up back to potential next year. What do you think does that, and does monetary policy help do that?

Williams: From my perspective, hopefully all these things come together in a good way in the sense that inflation will pick up some this year due to the effects of tariffs. Then we'll see inflation coming down next year and reaching our 2% goal in 2027.

I think the economy will slow a bit this year but then come back more to [a] trend [growth rate] and then monetary policy over that full period will be moving to neutral. One of the reasons I do expect growth to pick up next year is some of the uncertainties that were so prevalent early in the year are receding; they are already receding somewhat and hopefully, will continue to do so.

Businesses are adjusting to the economic environment, including some of the tariffs that have been put in place. I also see financial conditions broadly as being quite supportive of growth. You look at broad indices or measures of financial conditions, they're really positive in terms of wealth for households, positive in terms of businesses' cost of funds and things like that.

So there's a number of positive factors. I will just say, I know this is a big news story all the time, but from the macro point of view, I think that the fact that the U.S. continues to be the leader of new technologies including artificial intelligence is just really important for thinking about the underlying strength of the U.S. economy. So I feel like those are, if you will, some of the tailwinds behind us as we come out of this year.

Timiraos: Is 2.8% core inflation, maybe 2.5% if you are inclined to take tariffs out, consistent with meeting your target? Are you continuing to make progress toward your target?

Williams: So those are two different questions and I'm going to answer differently. So first of all, I agree, we have 2.8% core inflation in the PCE price index through the latest data. I think trying to estimate the effects of tariffs is difficult; it requires a lot of analysis and assumptions.

But I think as a first cut, the way you described it, I would agree with core inflation running around 2.5% based on some estimates of what the effects of tariffs have been on imported goods prices. So that's not 2% inflation. I think that core inflation is still meaningfully above 2%. So that is one of the reasons I think we need modestly restrictive monetary policy. It will help bring inflation back to 2% on a sustained basis.

On the second question, have we seen improvements in underlying inflation trends? I would say we have. We've seen core inflation and inflation come down quite a bit over the past few years. Inflation moves can be noisy. It moves up and down for various reasons.

But I think that again, doing this adjusting for the effects that we're seeing recently in tariffs, we have seen, in core inflation, over the last couple of years move, albeit in a bumpy way, lower and towards our target.

I actually feel that the overall data on inflation has been very, in a way, positive in the sense that I see continuing improvements in housing inflation and also in core services excluding housing. But we're not at the target, we're not at 2% yet.

Timiraos: On tariffs, if the price effects from higher import duties are more lagged and maybe more attenuated than some people had thought earlier this year, what implications would that have for demand management authorities, particularly now that you also have the prospect for a little bit of fiscal expansion over the next six to 12 months?

Williams: I think that clearly understanding the effects of tariffs on inflation and on the economy are really important just so that we have a good read of what's happening in the economy and where it's likely to go.

So I think it's an important factor and to the extent that in the end, the effects of tariffs on the economy are smaller, that means I think we get closer to the underlying arc, if you will, of the economy that we had entered the year with which is solid growth, the economy doing well and inflation coming down back to 2% of the next two years.

In a way that's a scenario that, from my point of view, puts us back closer to the path that we would've been on otherwise in terms of the economy. The trick here though is always trying to understand in real time what these effects are because they are both direct and indirect effects on that.

And what is important is not just the tariffs. I know we pay a lot of attention to this. It's natural because it's an unusual circumstance; we want to understand it and that's absolutely critical. But what's equally or maybe even more important is also understanding what's happening in the economy, abstracting a bit from the tariffs.

So understanding what's happening with the underlying inflation, which I just mentioned, or what's happening in the labor market in the economy. And when you mentioned fiscal policy, I see that as a modest tailwind. For us, it's not a big game changer. A lot of that was, I think, expected but is in the near term some additional tailwind for the growth for next year.

Timiraos: On net, does that have any implication for monetary policy?

Williams: For me, it's not any of the individual things. It's really how do they all fit together. And that's going back to the totality of the data. I personally think what matters for monetary policy is that we position policy as well as we can to achieve our dual goals and up until our dual mandate goals.

And up until now, up until recently, inflation has clearly been the primary concern and we've been at something close to maximum employment or as best we can measure that for some time. And that's very good. As we move forward, I'm hoping that we'll see continued progress in the underlying inflation and also want to make sure that we're getting a good balance of making sure we're achieving maximum employment as well as we can.

So I think what we're going to see, to your monetary policy question, is what we may well see, at least in my baseline forecast, is hopefully the underlying inflation moves in the right direction and watching how the labor market and seeing how that balancing act plays out in terms of achieving our goals.

Again, in my baseline view of how this will play out, over time, eventually, we will want to see monetary policy move back to a more neutral setting. It is currently modestly restrictive and that's completely appropriate given that underlying inflation is still running above our 2% goal.

But when we see inflation come back [down, and consistent] with the outlook where inflation is coming back to 2%, we'll eventually see monetary policy be closer to neutral.

Timiraos: Given the frequently stated desire to be forward-looking in setting policy and given some of the lags of not just monetary policy but generalized policy uncertainty, do you need a restrictive policy, still?

Williams: I would describe it as modestly restrictive. So let me give you a couple data points that can frame that. So I mentioned if you look at a broad set of labor market indicators, not just the unemployment rate, but if you look at quits and vacancies and perceptions of the labor market, we've seen a modest cooling over the last past 12 months and I think that's been helping us.

It's still a solid labor market, but it is helping to make sure that the labor market, the state of the economy is not adding unwelcome inflationary pressures. The second side of this modestly restrictive policy is I would argue we have seen a modest slowing of underlying inflation over the past year. And so those are two signs.

We're not talking about a sharp decline in inflation or sharp deterioration [of the] labor market. We're saying modestly restrictive policy has helped move us over time closer to achieving our goals. And so that's the way I think about it, is the modestly restrictive [interest rate stance] is really just trying to ease us along on that path to achieve our goals.

The question ahead of us is not whether we need modestly restrictive [policy] or not, but you can also turn that dial a little bit. You could say, well, not as restrictive, a little bit less restrictive and eventually, at some point, maybe get all the way to something that seems more neutral.

So I think that the policy decisions ahead of us are, at least initially, really, about 'We have a modestly restrictive stance. It's working the way we want. And if that continues to be true, is it time to turn that dial down a little bit?' I'm talking about on the baseline, assuming nothing changes fundamentally.

Timiraos: I know you just had a meeting, and there's a lot of time and data to study before the next meeting, but if you were to reduce policy rates by 25 basis points in seven weeks, policy would, by many measures, still be restrictive. Why not go ahead and do that?

Williams: It's really about getting the balance right. Currently, inflation is above our 2% inflation goal, has been above our goal for a number of years. The labor market still is solid in my view. And so it is really about having, I think — the data in its totality that informs what is that telling us not only about the current level of inflation but what the outlook for inflation is over the next year or two.

And also telling us what's the state of the labor market and what the risks are to that. And I think at the end of the day, it's really about a risk assessment. Are the risks to achieving sustained 2% inflation versus the risk to a deterioration of the labor market? So getting that would move us away from maximum employment.

So to me, it's not about a, it's not an absolute question. We're in a situation where inflation is above our target, labor market is still solid, but there are risks to those and we want to get that balance. So I think of it more as a how do you calibrate the policy and the best way to achieve those goals.

Timiraos: Your colleague, [San Francisco Fed President] Mary Daly, had implied there's some tension between waiting to see the impacts of tariffs on inflation over time and the worry that that would risk unnecessary damage to the labor market. If the economy is being held back by restrictive policy, how do you see that trade-off?

Williams: We always have to be forward-looking in policymaking, and we also always have to make decisions without complete information. So I think that as we assess the data and assess what the data are telling us about the outlook and maybe some of the risks of the outlook, we have to take in all of that in together in thinking about the stance of policy.

So I do agree that I think the monetary policy does take some time to have its full effects on monetary policy lags. And so we always have to be forward-looking and we can't just be looking and saying, well, where are we today? But what's the likely paths of the economy and what's the uncertainties around that and how does that relate to the risks? To me, it's not about 'I need to wait until I have all the information to know for sure.'

That's not how I think about policy. I think it's not how most people think about policy. It's about having a sufficiently good understanding of where things are and likely to go to be able to make a decision in a specific direction. And again, it's a decision that we come back in six more weeks and say, OK, let's now reassess, re-evaluate what's the next decision to make.

And one of the things about monetary policy is we're not making decisions for the next three years. We're making the decision for today. We collect data over the intermediate period. We reconvene and then can come back and say, 'OK, given what we've learned now, what's the right next step?' And then do that again.

I think that's an important part of that. And I think honestly the markets, they understand that and that's why markets respond to the information and try to understand not just what's our next decision, but what's that mean for policy down the road.

Timiraos: Well, since you're bringing up markets reacting, I know you may not want to bless any particular judgment by the market, but expectations for a September rate cut have moved substantially higher this morning — up to 80% before we started this interview. Does that seem unreasonable to you given the current outlook? Williams: I'm going to agree with your first point. I'm not going to bless the movement of the markets. I think I will say one thing that I've watched over the past few years is the market participants are confronted with the same challenging questions that we are as policymakers, what's happening with the economy, what's happening with inflation, what's happening with growth, consumer spending, the labor market, what's happening globally around the world.

And so we're all trying to sift through all that information and come to conclusions. Now, we have, as policymakers, our goal is not to price securities or assets. Our job is to focus on achieving our maximum employment and price stability goals. So I will say that markets are responding to signals in ways that directionally — let me say that — directionally, I think are understandable. And I'm not saying that they're right or wrong.

I think that they're trying to do that. Of course, as you pointed out earlier, we've gotten quite a bit of data since the FOMC meeting [on Wednesday]. I know it's only two days ago, but we've gotten quite a bit of data that's relevant, but we're going to get a lot more data between now and then.

And I think all of us and all the people who, in financial markets, will be assessing each of those pieces of data not only in isolation, but it's how does it fit together because no one piece of data is telling you the full picture. It's how they all fit together.

Timiraos: Would a more diffused or elongated process of absorbing tariff costs into the retail price chain make you more or less willing to remove policy restriction? How does that cut?

Williams: It's a really interesting question and I think there's two sides of it. One is if you just asked me if I knew exactly how everything was going to play out, like we're sitting here at a board game and saying, well, it's the more diffuse, but still what's going to happen?

I think that in a way that probably creates less upside risks to inflation or inflation expectations, maybe not as worried about second-round effects and things like that. So that may be, OK, it's a small shock. Small shocks can be absorbed into the economy over time and we've seen that. In my career, that's been a normal thing.

So I definitely think that shocks that have run through gradually and things maybe not as abrupt or perhaps don't create as much uncertainty and risk. So on the other side is the inferring what's happening issue. Because we saw that a little bit even last month where there was some commentary out there a month ago that we hadn't seen through May, we hadn't seen any effects of tariffs and well, they're not going to have effects.

And I was like, well, wait a minute, hold on there. Just because we haven't seen them yet doesn't mean we won't. And I made that point in a recent speech as did many others. And then of course the June data comes and said, OK, now we're seeing them. And I think that's where there's always the uncertainty about if we see small effects for a while, does that mean we're always going to see small effects or is it just the lags are longer?

And to me, it is more just maybe it's the humility around assessing and evaluating the effects of some of these things on the actual data. I think we've all learned that a couple of things have happened with the tariffs. First of all, there's the lag between the announcement which we all see and then the actual implementation.

And then we've seen also that the measure I used or referred to in my speech was the tariff for revenue as a share of imports has risen much more slowly than the announced effective tariff rates. And that reflects a number of factors and there could be other factors at play, too, about lagged effects of tariffs on actual data.

Again, going back to the humility, we have to recognize that with something that's like this, it could be that it happens at a different pace than we expect. The magnitudes may be different and the added uncertainty is probably not going to go away for a while. And so we just again, don't want to draw really strong conclusions from one month of data about its effects.

Timiraos: Are inflation expectations anchored?

Williams: I would say inflation expectations are remarkably well anchored and that's a very important thing. One-year-ahead inflation expectations like inflation swaps definitely are elevated. That doesn't seem surprising to me given some of these tariff effects are likely to increase prices over the next year. I don't think anyone would be surprised by that. And I think the markets expect increases in prices from the tariffs that have been announced that are coming into play.

What we see though is if you go one year further out on the inflation swap, so that's a one-year, one year forward. So for year two, inflation expectations come back closer to something close to our 2% inflation goal. You see that with break-even inflation from the TIPS market. So the market-based measures and inflation expectations show some bump, if you will, in inflation over the next 12 months, but really not much after that.

So that's how the markets are at least perceiving it. And the survey of consumer expectations that the New York Fed runs, we saw inflation expectations pick up earlier in the year but actually have come back down. So I see that as reassuring and longer-term inflation expectations in our survey didn't move at all.

So I think that looking at most of the evidence from surveys, from markets, economists, I think inflation expectations are very well-anchored. You didn't ask the question. I feel like you're going to. So how worried am I about persistent inflation coming out of the tariffs or other things that have been going on?

I think that we are not seeing any signs that either consumers or businesses or markets are expecting some persistently elevated inflation. So what I am focused on, I'm not so much worried about that happening. I'm very focused on making sure monetary policy is well-positioned, the stance of policy is well-positioned, to make sure that we're not in any way contributing to imbalances in the economy, in one direction or the other, that could lead to some inflation dynamics that would be unwelcome.

So I would say that my baseline case is that inflation will come back down next year and then move back to 2% and that's all good. But we have to make sure that our monetary policy is well positioned to keep the economy in a good balance.

Timiraos: Some of your colleagues have already concluded this is going to be a one-off and you should set aside whatever goods price increases you see. That obviously simplifies things a lot, when you're ready to do that. But it sounds like you're saying that if you do ease too soon, you could create an equilibrium that you regret.

Williams: I would say that I think that the economics, that the tariffs or other factors like that in general, have effects on the price level and do not create inflation for five years. I think this is correct. But I think it needs to be reinforced or complemented with policies that keep the economy in good balance and don't contribute to imbalances that could cause inflation to become more persistent. And also, policies that are well positioned to keep inflation expectations longer.

I think we're in a good place on this. I guess when you think about the increase in inflation expectations, we just need to make sure that we're not, in any way, doing anything that would disturb the well-incorporated inflation expectations that we have.

Timiraos: On the labor market, there's been a lot of focus on the breadth of hiring.

Williams: Yes.

Timiraos: How much of this reflects perhaps catch-up effects from the pandemic reopening process when some businesses just couldn't compete as well as others or some industries couldn't compete as well for workers as others, and so to some extent we're seeing a catch-up effect?

Williams: There is some of that. I definitely think there has been that over the past year or two. But I also think that this is a measure I'm watching closely, so I'll come back to this often, called the diffusion index in the labor market reports.

So what we heard from businesses, whether through data analysis, or just talking to people during the peak hot labor market of '21 and '22, is that there were certain employers who could afford to pay higher salaries and significantly higher salaries. They could mark to the market in the compensation they were offering and other employers were constrained in their ability to do that.

Think about government, think about the healthcare industry and in education. There were a number of sectors who just didn't have the ability to compete as well as some of the employers during that period. So what we saw was a lot of employment gains and churn in the labor market as people, honestly, moved to higher-paying jobs and took advantage of that very hot labor market. As the labor market has cooled and got more in balance, that dynamic I think has disappeared, honestly.

We're seeing churn in the labor market is actually relatively low. We're having the low-hire, low-fire economy now, which I think is an important thing I'll come back to. But it also means that some of those employers who found themselves unable to compete and just running the organization's staffing way below what they wanted have been able over the past year or two to be able to fill those positions.

So we have seen a lot of hiring in healthcare, education, and in state and local government. So I think that dynamic is relevant to understanding part of this. So when people have said to me, well, all the hiring is really at least a couple sectors, I said, well, that's not surprising given what I've seen in the past few years.

But let me now roll it into even the most recent data and with the revised data that came out today. Definitely, as we've seen this economy slow in terms of the hire rate, in terms of the number of job gains per month, clearly, we're seeing the number of sectors or whatever professions that are not seeing job growth. And so we are definitely seeing more concentration in the few sectors.

So I think that that first dynamic I talked about is still in play. But I think the second thing that's in play is that we are in this very low hiring and low firing economy. It's a low churn economy, which is so different from three years ago. And I think that's implying that we're seeing less — we're not seeing the broad-based job gains, we're seeing them more focused in certain areas, but we're not really seeing much job gains at all in many of the sectors.

So it affects how the indexes are constructed, but we're also not seeing the layoffs. You look at layoff data, you look at job separations, those are relatively low too. So I think that explains the dynamic that the diffusion measures are relatively low over the past few months and I think it is both of those things coming together.

That said, a diffusion index about job gains across the various categories has been historically one of the indicators of the state of the economy. It is something I definitely am watching carefully.

Timiraos: There seems to be some disagreement on the committee around how restrictive current policy is, which implies disagreement on the short-run neutral rate. How does this settle out for you? Presumably fiscal policy and financial conditions have been raising the short-run neutral rate?

Williams: I find the short-run neutral rate kind of a tricky concept and try to avoid interpreting it that way because as you just said, and I think it's right, we have fiscal policy affecting the near-term tailwinds, headwinds of the economy and of financial conditions.

We have a lot of factors, domestically and globally, that affect the near-term supply and demand of our economy. And so when I think of neutral rates, whether for the labor market or for the interest rates, I prefer to think of those as a longer-term development.

So let me just take your question, but I am not avoiding your question. I want to go back to how I think about is policy modestly restrictive? It's not by saying what's the real rate relative to a long-run 'r-star,' [or the neutral rate of interest] because I think that's a measure that's useful to think more medium term than the near term.

I'm just looking at our two goal variables, maximum employment and price stability. And I mentioned earlier that the labor market over the past year has been relatively stable. But when I look closely, I see a modest cooling across a number of measures outside of the unemployment rate.

And then when I look at core inflation and underlying measures of inflation, I see a modest cooling. Again, different measures, a little bit different ways or different indications of this, but so I feel like if you were to say, and I don't want to call this a short-run r-star, but if you were to say in the near term, how is monetary policy positioned relative to all the forces affecting the economy? Monetary policy is positioned in a way that has been consistent with and supporting this process of a gradual modest cooling of the labor market and a gradual movement of underlying inflation back to 2%. That's how I think about it.

If you want to ask me about longer run, I think that it's a distinct question because some of these — in the near term over this year, next year, the short-run factors are the dominant ones affecting the economy, not these longer ones.

Timiraos: At various points in time, policymakers, including your predecessor here, have suggested that a large balance sheet provided stimulus and thus raised the equilibrium rate. Do you agree with that framing? Is it still valid? And if so, does that imply a longer balance sheet runoff or a smaller target level of reserves would allow you to lower the funds rate more?

Williams: This is a question, as you mentioned, that has been debated and discussed for a long time. It really depends on how you think about the balance sheet, its effect on, really, prices of Treasury securities, the term premium for Treasury securities or other securities related.

And then how does that then feed back into the effect on aggregate demand and then on the neutral interest rate? So I think, conceptually, I've always felt that there's a logic to an argument, that a larger balance sheet adds some stimulus over the medium term to the economy and that means that monetary policy in terms of short-term interest rates, doesn't need to add the same stimulus to achieve equilibrium.

I think quantitatively it's just really difficult to know how big those effects are because first, you have to have a view on the effects of the size of the balance sheet on the term premium. And then how big is that effect on the macroeconomy over the medium term? And I don't have that much confidence in that.

So often, researchers look at things like, well, let's not look at the Fed's balance sheet or balance sheets, but let's just look at the term premium or the convenience yield or other measures in financial markets about the relative pricing of Treasury securities versus other assets. And maybe just look at that because that is affected by a lot of things besides the size of the balance sheet.

As a practical matter, the FOMC has clearly and consistently said the primary instrument of monetary policy is the federal-funds rate. We've used the balance sheet and our tool kit when needed due to the circumstances we faced during the pandemic and following that.

So I view that our strategy is, as we've articulated very clearly, let's reduce the balance sheet in a way that's consistent with effective implementation of monetary policy, operate that effectively and well and don't think of that as, well, this is a separate tool for monetary policy.

And then given that we do that well, and that is going very smoothly, the reduction of our balance sheet, that monetary policy is just focused on the federal-funds rate, part of monetary policy is focused on macroeconomic stabilization and achieving our mandate goals.

Timiraos: It sounds like the Fed is going to address its framework review at the Jackson Hole conference with the major shift moving away from the idea of average inflation targeting. Will that shift have any practical implications for the conduct of policy going forward?

Williams: I am not going to opine on what we may or may not do. As the chair has articulated very clearly, we've had a really, really good process at the committee thinking through this review, and that will be shared publicly later.

From my point of view, I feel that since 2012 — and I was fortunate to be on the committee in January 2012 when we first approved the long-run goals and monetary policy strategy document — through the iterations of that, including the changes in 2020, we have always been squarely focused on maximum employment and price stability, squarely focused on anchoring inflation expectations as the foundation of achieving maximum employment and price stability.

And I feel that that foundational view of the importance of achieving our goals and how to do that has been just incredibly important. And I expect that that will continue to be the case.

Timiraos: To sum up then, if the data were to reveal itself over the next two months in a way consistent with what we've seen over the last few months, which I would characterize as some goods inflation filtering through, maybe not as much or as fast as people had thought, and the labor market that doesn't appear to be getting any stronger, but again, hard to read because of changes in supply and demand. Would you be prepared to support resuming rate normalization as soon as your next meeting?

Williams: I'm not going to predict the 'as soon as your next meeting' part. But I will say that I believe firmly in being data dependent and assessing all of the totality of all the data, what it's telling us about the economy, the likely path of the economy and the risks to achieving our goals.

And if the data are progressing in a way consistent with the best achievement of our goals — and to achieve that, and balance the risks best, it makes sense to reduce the restrictiveness of monetary policy, then that's what we should do. So from my mind, it's not just about setting interest rates. It's about what is it that we should do in order to achieve our goals.

I do think if you look at the SEP for June, there's a range of views on that and I think that those range, personally, I'm not speaking for my colleagues or anybody, I think that a range of views captures very much the set of issues that we face. There's quite a bit of uncertainty.

We are, in many ways, getting close to achieving our goals. We just need to get the job done and do it really well for the benefit of the American people. And I feel like really now it's just assessing that information and getting to the right decision. And so I think that I go into this with very much an open mind.

Timiraos: A lot of people look at all the pressure that the administration and the president have been putting on the central bank and they say, 'Well, this must make policymakers want to push back a little bit.' If I shoved you into the wall, all of the muscles on that side of your body would tense up. Does that happen when there are these sorts of pressures being placed on you? Williams: Absolutely not. It is striking. I've been in the Fed a long time and throughout my career I've been just so impressed with all of my colleagues, all of my colleagues, that they come in, each and every one comes in the room having read all the analysis, studied all the data, thought through all the issues, heard all the pros and cons of every issue, comes into the room, shares what they've learned.

We obviously share what we're hearing from around the country, all the different perspectives and just try to get this down to the things we only have: maximum employment, price stability, those are our goals, how do we best achieve that? And, literally, the rest of the world is shut out of the room. It's always been that way. It continues to be that way. That's why we choose to take these roles, so that we can carry out this one very important task of monetary policy and do it as well as we can.

The thing about our jobs as policymakers is there's no confusion about people don't like inflation. People really don't like inflation, but people obviously suffer if it's a weak economy, we don't achieve our maximum employment goals. Both of these are critically important for the welfare of the American people. And so we just have to do our very best to get it right.

And as the data surprises you, it moves in ways that you don't expect, to move with that and just stay focused. I just feel it is a remarkable institution in terms of, you could say it's nerdy, you can call it a lot of things, but it's just so focused on getting the best analysis based on evidence and trying to make the best decision that we can and come back in six or seven weeks and do it again.

data.title: We're Seeing a 'Gentle Gradual Cooling of the Labor Market' Over the Past Year, Fed's Williams Says — WSJ
New York Fed's Williams Says He Will Go Into September Meeting With 'Open Mind' on Cutting Rates — WSJ
Fed's Williams: Unusually Large Downward Revisions to May, June Job Gains Were 'Really the News' of Friday's Report — WSJ
Fed's Williams: Natural to Have Divided Views Given Elevated Uncertainty Over Range of Changes on Trade, Immigration, Fiscal Policy — WSJ
Fed's Williams: 'The Labor Market Still Is Solid in My View' — WSJ
Fed's Williams Sees Tailwinds Developing in 2026, Including Dominant Position on New AI Technology — WSJ
Fed's Williams Sees Economic Growth Slowing to Around 1% This Year — WSJ
Fed's Williams Sees Economic Growth Rebounding in 2026 as Policy Uncertainty, Other Headwinds Fade — WSJ
Fed Officials Take Cool Jobs Report in Stride — WSJ
data.description:
By Nick Timiraos

July's jobs report rattled financial markets and meaningfully raised the prospect that the Federal Reserve will cut interest rates at its next meeting in September.

But two Fed officials on Friday maintained that the U.S. labor market is still well balanced, describing the latest softness as part of a gradual cooling rather than a worrying deterioration.

In an interview Friday, New York Fed President John Williams said he would go into the September meeting with "very much an open mind" about cutting interest rates. But he also offered a more measured assessment that tempered the sense of urgency driving expectations for more aggressive easing.

"What we're seeing I would describe over the past year as a gentle gradual cooling of the labor market, but still leaving it in a still solid place," said Williams, a top ally of Fed Chair Jerome Powell.

Fed officials were split over their decision to hold rates steady at their meeting on Wednesday, with two officials voting in favor of a cut. Nine others supported the decision to hold steady.

Even among those who favored taking no action in July, a divide is emerging over whether to worry more about the labor market or the prospect for firmer inflation in the months ahead, creating a challenge for Powell in forging a consensus going forward.

While the unemployment rate rose only modestly in July to 4.2%, reversing a decline to 4.1% in June, soggier payroll figures give Powell room to build agreement for a rate decrease because they undercut a narrative that the labor market has strong momentum. Officials will see one more month of job data before their next meeting.

Unusually large downward revisions to job gains in May and June had been "really the news of this report," said Williams. "This is important information...to understand the direction of what we're seeing in supply and demand for labor" and the labor market's cooler momentum.

A wider range of indicators, including job openings and initial claims for jobless benefits, paint a picture of an economy that remains in the same slow-to-hire, slow-to-fire equilibrium that's been a feature for the past year, Williams said. "The labor market still is solid in my view," he said.

Cleveland Fed President Beth Hammack echoed that assessment. In an interview Friday with Bloomberg Television, she said the labor market "looks like a healthy labor market that's still well in balance, but with some disappointing signs that we should watch very carefully." She added that weaker job growth makes sense given reduced immigration.

White House economic adviser Stephen Miran said Friday's jobs report was "a disappointment" but attributed much of the weakness to technical and temporary factors.

In an interview, he said about 60% of the downward revisions to May and June job gains were due to seasonal adjustment issues. Policy uncertainty around tariffs had temporarily weighed on hiring, but its resolution with recently announced trade deals should allow job growth to rebound in coming months, he said.

Williams said the concentration of job gains in certain sectors like healthcare, education and government partly reflects a catch-up dynamic from the pandemic era. During the hot labor market of 2021-22, employers who could afford to pay higher salaries competed aggressively for workers, while others — particularly government agencies, healthcare systems and schools — "just didn't have the ability to compete as well" and ran understaffed.

As the labor market has cooled, those employers have been able to fill positions they couldn't during the hiring frenzy, he said.

Williams answered questions about a possible September cut cautiously, declining to defend market expectations that at one point on Friday placed an 80% probability on a September rate cut.

"Market participants are confronted with the same challenging questions that we are as policymakers," Williams said. Markets are "responding to signals in ways that directionally...I think are understandable."

"The question ahead of us is not whether we need" to maintain a modestly restrictive interest-rate policy, but rather about fine-tuning the level of restriction, he said, as it moves gradually to a more neutral stance. "Is it time to turn that dial down a little bit?" he said.

Fed governors Michelle Bowman and Christopher Waller, who dissented in favor of a rate cut at Wednesday's meeting, issued statements before Friday's report warning of weaker labor market conditions if the central bank didn't resume the rate cuts it began last year.

Williams said it was natural to have divided views, given elevated uncertainty over a range of changes on trade, immigration and fiscal policy.

Williams's measured approach reflects his view that the Fed still has work to do on inflation. He noted that a widely watched gauge that excludes volatile food and energy prices is "still meaningfully above" the Fed's 2% target.

While he said businesses' and consumers' expectations of inflation had been holding at low and stable levels, he said the Fed still had an important job to do in making sure that remained true. "It needs to be reinforced or complemented with policies that keep the economy in good balance," he said.

Williams expects economic growth to slow to around 1% this year but said he expects growth to rebound in 2026 as policy uncertainty and other headwinds fade and as tailwinds develop, including America's dominant position on new artificial-intelligence technology.

"I'm not...particularly worried right now about the economy contracting or really weakening a lot," Williams said. "It has slowed, but I expect it to just be at this pace of growth for a couple quarters and then come back."

Write to Nick Timiraos at Nick.Timiraos@wsj.com

data.title: The Leaner, Meaner Chevron Is Ready to Fight — WSJ
data.description:
By Collin Eaton

Chief Executive Mike Wirth had a stern message for Chevron's 40,000 employees in February: Stop being so nice to each other.

Leaders need to be more decisive, take accountability for failures and have uncomfortable conversations about poor performance, Wirth and his lieutenants said during a virtual companywide meeting. A change in attitude could keep Chevron from falling behind its competitors, they said, according to people who attended the meeting.

The effort to overhaul Chevron's corporate culture emanated from a report the company commissioned from consulting firm McKinsey that found employees were frustrated. The company wasn't innovative, and it lacked a clear strategy, they said.

"We are 'nice' even when desired results or behaviors are not achieved," according to McKinsey's summary of employee feedback, which was reviewed by The Wall Street Journal. "We emphasize collaboration and inclusion to build strong teams...but we do not have direct, uncomfortable conversations needed to deliver results."

Chevron executives turned to McKinsey last year as its shares lagged behind rival Exxon Mobil's amid headwinds in key parts of its oil business. Its major oil project in Kazakhstan had hit costly snags. As oil prices moderated, drilling slowed in the Permian Basin, the largest U.S. oil field. And, most notably, its $53 billion acquisition of Hess was stalled after a shocking move by Exxon to try to intervene in the deal.

Now, the $310 billion oil giant, the second largest in the U.S. behind Exxon, is getting tougher not just on its employees but also on rivals, political detractors and anyone else it encounters.

After outlining a sweeping reorganization of its business units on the February call, Chevron announced plans to cut roughly 8,000 jobs, or up to 20% of its workforce, through 2026, part of a plan to save up to $3 billion. And two weeks ago, the Hess deal finally closed.

"I'm incredibly proud of Chevron's culture and wouldn't trade it for anybody else's," Wirth said in a statement to The Wall Street Journal this past week. "We're working to further strengthen our culture with an even sharper focus on performance by executing faster and more efficiently, simplifying our organization, and delivering targeted innovation."

For over a century, Chevron was California's quintessential oil company, fostering a more genteel and mellow corporate environment than that of its bigger rival, the more rigid and often litigious Exxon of Texas. Chevron had long been considered by industry veterans the more comfortable, collaborative place to work. It still aims to adhere to principles that boil down to treating people fairly, but with a sharper focus on delivery.

Chevron's corporate history traces back to 1876, when wildcatters drilled California's first successful oil well in the Santa Susana Mountains, north of Los Angeles. The subsequent oil boom drove the economic development of California. Chevron eventually became the state's largest employer, with its logo on every other gas station, its name in opera playbills and museum brochures, and its leaders enjoying easy access to the state's governors and lawmakers.

In the 1990s, Chevron embraced a movement that was popular in California and became the first major oil company to include sexual orientation in its equal-employment-opportunity policies. It extended benefits to same-sex couples, despite opposition in other places where it operated like Texas and Mississippi.

Wirth, 64, has been an engineer and Chevron employee for over 40 years and rose to chairman and CEO in 2018. At the time, Chevron was facing cost overruns on large projects. Wirth was seen as a cost-conscious executive, having led the company's refining and chemicals business — in which margins are critical — for almost a decade.

The deal for Hess, first announced in October 2023, was seen by Wall Street as critical to securing Chevron's long-term oil reserves and profits while cementing Wirth's legacy as a transformational leader. Instead, Exxon stepped in and essentially said not so fast. It asserted a right of first refusal to bid for Hess's 30% stake in a generational oil discovery in Guyana — a move that stunned Chevron executives and the oil community at large.

Exxon has a 45% stake in the project in Guyana and serves as the operator of vessels pumping about 650,000 barrels of oil a day there. By 2027, daily production is expected to reach 1.2 million barrels, making it one of the most coveted projects in the world as many oil fields deplete and new exploration can take decades to pay off.

For nearly two years, Chevron's acquisition of Hess hung in limbo while the companies battled over the Guyana crown jewel.

Early on, analysts, bankers and investors predicted Chevron would negotiate some offering to Exxon — perhaps a percentage of Hess's stake, for instance- — to avoid arbitration.

Chevron held firm. It never restarted talks after Exxon filed for arbitration, even as it waited more than a year for the process to begin.

Meanwhile, investors were clamoring about Chevron's growth prospects without Hess and questioning whether it would need to land another megadeal if Exxon prevailed in the dispute.

Losing Hess would have marked the second major transaction that Wirth failed to close as CEO. He backed away from a potential bidding war with Occidental Petroleum in 2019 over Anadarko Petroleum, a deal that saddled Occidental with debt.

Chevron ultimately prevailed in the arbitration dispute last month — but it wasn't without some hard feelings.

"It has been a long process, and it didn't need to be," Wirth said in an interview July 18, the day Chevron closed the Hess acquisition. "It should have been resolved quicker. The outcome was never in doubt."

"After 2027, it was very murky if they had anything in the funnel," Jean Ann Salisbury, an analyst at Bank of America, said of Chevron. Now, with the Hess deal completed, Chevron is positioned to "close much of the gap" with Exxon.

To do that, Chevron is aiming to become leaner and meaner.

Exxon has long been known as a merit-driven organization that doesn't shy away from a fight. With its army of lawyers and its extensive time in court — most often because of lawsuits filed against it by cities and states over climate change — it has been likened by some to a law firm that produces oil.

Exxon suffered a rare stumble during the pandemic when the company booked a $20 billion loss for 2020. Chevron's market value briefly eclipsed Exxon's for the first time that October. Later, Exxon lost a bruising proxy fight to a little-known activist investor, prompting its executives to double down on protecting their turf. Last year, the company sued two sustainability investors for making climate-related shareholder proposals. The investment firms backed off, and at this year's annual meeting, Exxon encountered no shareholder proposals for the first time since 1958.

Exxon's move to block the deal between Chevron and Hess was especially pugnacious. Analysts said Exxon had little to lose — and was successful on some level by stalling Chevron's capture of a slice of Guyana's riches.

Wirth and his lieutenants at Chevron aren't letting anyone stand in their way.

Wirth moved Chevron's corporate headquarters last year from California, where it had resided for more than 140 years. Relations had soured with the state government. In moving to Houston, Chevron joined Exxon and much of the rest of the industry.

Throughout its history, Chevron had "attracted a lot of people who were from California or went to college in California," which contributed to a more open-minded and neighborly attitude at the company, said Edward Chow, a Chevron executive for over 20 years until 1999. "Now, to me, it feels more like another oil company. The distinction between Exxon and Chevron has narrowed in terms of corporate culture."

By the time Wirth decamped for more fossil-friendly Texas from Chevron's campus in San Ramon, east of San Francisco, Chevron's relationship with the Golden State and Democratic Gov. Gavin Newsom had broken down.

California acted more aggressively than any other state to steer consumers away from fossil fuels and claimed oil companies were price gouging. In response, Chevron's executives launched a public-relations campaign in California that blamed lawmakers for the state's high prices. The campaign included big signs with QR codes at Chevron gas stations that urged drivers to complain directly to lawmakers starting around 2022.

In November, Wirth said California's state government was putting bureaucrats in charge of key parts of the economy, a move that "hasn't worked in other socialist states."

In January, about three weeks after President Trump suggested the U.S. should rename the Gulf of Mexico the Gulf of America, Wirth told investors Chevron would begin calling the region the Gulf of America. "That's the position of the U.S. government now," he said.

Wirth has built strong ties with several officials in the Trump administration. The oil industry has enlisted the administration in fights against clean-car rules, drilling restraints and climate laws from New York to California. Meanwhile, the financial world has all but turned away from the environmental concerns that pervaded banks and big investment firms in the Biden years, easing pressure on oil producers. Executives are trying to use their political sway to push for the U.S. government to streamline permitting for pipelines and encourage fossil-fuel export deals.

Chevron's lobbying efforts with President Trump and his cabinet secretaries paid off again last month, this time in South America.

In May, the Trump administration pulled Chevron's Biden-era license to pump oil in Venezuela to clamp down on socialist dictator Nicolás Maduro.

data.title: U.S. Stock Market Loses Over $1 Trillion—Is Bitcoin About to Follow?
data.description:
U.S. stock markets saw a staggering wipeout, losing over $1 trillion in market value as soaring inflation and sweeping new trade tariffs triggered widespread investor panic. The Dow plunged 1.2%, the S&P 500 slid 1.6%, and the Nasdaq dropped 2.2%, contributing to the multi-hundred-billion-dollar decline across equities.

Against this backdrop, Bitcoin also came under pressure—dipping shortly below $130,000 USD, with reports indicating a sharp move past the $115,000 level as markets turned risk-averse.

According to Coinglass data, more than $1 billion in liquidations occurred across the crypto derivatives market within the past 24 hours, intensifying the downturn. The liquidation wave wiped out both long and short positions as investors scrambled amid the volatility.

data.title: Trump says nobody has asked him to grant clemency to Ghislaine Maxwell
data.description:
U.S. President Donald Trump said on Friday that nobody had asked him to grant clemency to Ghislaine Maxwell, the British socialite serving a 20-year prison sentence for helping Jeffrey Epstein abuse underage girls.

"I'm allowed to do it, but nobody's asked me to do it. I know nothing about it. I don't know anything about the case, but I know I have the right to do it," Trump told Newsmax.

Trump also said he would not pardon Sean "Diddy" Combs, who was convicted in July on two charges of transportation to engage in prostitution.

data.title: Trump tells Newsmax that Powell will 'most likely' stay on as chair
data.description:
U.S. President Donald Trump said on Friday that Federal Reserve Chair Jerome Powell will "most likely" stay in his position even as Trump sharply criticized the Federal Reserve's policies.

In an interview with Newsmax that aired on Friday, Trump said he would remove Powell "in a heartbeat" and said the Fed's interest rate was too high but added that others have said Powell's removal would "disturb the market."

"He gets out in seven or eight months and I'll put somebody else in," Trump said.

data.title: Peruvian informal miners suspend talks with government due to disagreements in negotiations
data.description:
Informal miners in Peru suspended talks with the government and may resume protests due to disagreements in negotiations, one of the protest leaders said on Friday.

CONFEMIN union leader, Maximo Franco Bequer, told reporters the government refused to alter an August 17 deadline for miners to move explosives into formal "powder magazines."

He said 20,000 miners who cannot meet the deadline would be excluded from a government program to formalize their work.

The miners, located in the Cusco region, had suspended their two-week-long protest on July 15 that had blocked a major copper transit route used by mining firms MMG , Glencore and Hudbay .

Informal miners in Peru operate with temporary permits under a program created over a decade ago that the government has been trying to end. The miners say the stricter regulations to formalize their work and operate legally are too onerous and would likely leave them without employment.

The union expects to meet in the coming hours to decide on new protests.

data.title: New Issues Are Sizzling — Barrons.com
data.description:
By Paul R. La Monica

Unicorns may be mythical beasts, but multibillion-dollar start-ups are alive and well and running wild on Wall Street. Design software firm Figma is the latest company to make a splashy debut. It surged 250% from its initial-public-offering price when it began trading on July 31 and continued to rise a day later.

Figma's explosive start follows strong performances by other recent IPOs, such as stablecoin company Circle Internet Group, cloud computing and artificial-intelligence firm CoreWeave, fintech Chime, and drone maker AIRO.

Investors are clearly fascinated with companies that are expected to post hefty revenue gains at a time when tech and other momentum stocks are once again riding high. The Nasdaq Composite index isn't far from record levels, buoyed by the strong performance of Magnificent Seven members Nvidia, Microsoft, and Meta Platforms.

So what's next? The IPO market could soon succumb to its typical late-summer slowdown, but don't be surprised to see more companies looking to go public in the fall and into 2026. Matt Kennedy, senior IPO market strategist at Renaissance Capital, an IPO research and investing firm, said investors should keep an eye on space tech company Firefly Aerospace, which is expected to go public the week of Aug. 4, as well as buy now/pay later firm Klarna and ticket marketplace StubHub, which have already filed to go public.

Write to Paul R. La Monica at paul.lamonica@barrons.com

Last Week

Markets

The S&P 500 and the Nasdaq started the week extending their record runs. On Wednesday, the Federal Reserve kept interest rates unchanged. Two voting members dissented, a first in more than three decades. The economy expanded in the second quarter, with GDP rising a seasonally and inflation-adjusted 3% annual rate after contracting in the first quarter. Copper prices plunged after President Donald Trump announced 50% tariffs on some imports. On Friday, the markets swooned on Trump's blitz of tariffs and a weak jobs report; he ordered his team to fire the top Bureau of Labor Statistics official. On the week, the Nasdaq lost 2.2%, the S&P declined 2.4%, and the Dow Jones Industrial Average were down 2.9%.

Companies

In a week of Big Tech earnings, Microsoft posted double-digit revenue growth in its Azure cloud business and Meta Platforms smashed Wall Street forecasts. A gunman killed four people on Monday in a Midtown Manhattan office building, including a Blackstone senior real estate executive. Exxon Mobil and Chevron both posted better-than-expected earnings.

Deals

Norfolk Southern and Union Pacific plan a merger that will ease coast-to-coast freight travel...Oilfield-services company Baker Hughes said it's buying Chart Industries, which sells equipment for liquefied-natural-gas plants, for $13.6 billion...KKR acquired a majority stake in HealthCare Royalty Partners, which buys rights to pharmaceutical royalty streams.

Next Week

Monday 8/4

Tech and biotech firms kick off this week's calendar. On Semiconductor, BioNTech, and Axsome Therapeutics will report results before the market opens. After the close, Palantir Technologies and Vertex Pharmaceuticals will release their latest results.

Tuesday 8/5

Key chip stocks headline Tuesday's earnings wave. Advanced Micro Devices is scheduled to report fiscal second-quarter results after the market closes. Super Micro Computer will provide fourth-quarter and fiscal-year 2025 results.

Wednesday 8/6

Iconic American brands, both old and new, will keep earnings season rolling along on Wednesday. McDonald's and Uber Technologies will report second-quarter results before the market opens. After the market's close, Walt Disney will release its fiscal third-quarter results.

Thursday 8/7

The Federal Reserve reports consumer credit data for June, and the Department of Labor releases initial jobless claims for the week ending on Aug. 1.

The Numbers

$2 T

Likely revenue of the top six tech companies this year, equal to GDP of Russia, the 11th-largest economy.

14%

Increase in June-quarter sales of iPhones as some U.S. consumers rushed to buy ahead of tariffs.

30%

Portion of single-family home buyers who are investors looking to flip or rent, the highest share on record.

25%

Increase in assets in environmentally focused funds in the year through May, despite ESG's fall from favor.

Email: editors@barrons.com

This content was created by Barron's, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. Barron's is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal.

data.title: XRP Is Set To Move Trillions — Banking CEO Drops Bombshell On Ripple’s Role In Tokenizing Finance
data.description:
The Central banks, institutions, and payment corridors are experimenting with something deeper than crypto hype. From cross-border settlements to real-world assets, Ripple is building blockchain infrastructure. If recent comments from top banking executives are any indication, XRP may move trillions in value.

The Settlement Layer No One Saw Coming

According to KingXRP, the XRP Ledger (XRPL) is on the brink of a groundbreaking transformation that could unlock a staggering $196 trillion market through the emergence of RealFi and real-world finance powered by blockchain technology. His post includes a recent interview with Teucrium CEO Sal Gilbertie, where he made a bold declaration that XRP and Ripple will move trillions and tokenize the entire financial system. 

As mentioned in the video, XRP is often misunderstood. It can be traded and speculated on like any other asset, but its true purpose goes much deeper. Ripple is the company behind XRP, and it was originally founded to facilitate fast, efficient money transfers across borders. Apparently, XRP is now evolving far beyond simple transactions. It’s becoming a foundational tool for tokenizing a wide range of assets and enabling the movement of value in new and innovative ways. 

As Ripple continues to advance its level 2 infrastructure, it’s becoming increasingly clear that they are positioning to tokenize the entire financial system. They have acquired a broker-dealer clearing member, which is a strategic move that brings them one step closer to integrating deeply with the traditional financial markets.

This move marks what Gilbertie believes is the first step toward integrating traditional finance with DeFi. The acquisition of a broker-dealer isn’t just a technicality, but it’s the kind of infrastructure move that would rewire the system in the future.

XRP Moves Into Institutional Payment Rails

CryptoGeek has also shared an update on X about Ripple Chief Technology Officer (CTO), David Schwartz, stating that banks are now integrating XRP. Meanwhile, Ripple Bank will operate entirely on the XRP Ledger, settling all payments with XRP as its core asset. 

Schwartz emphasized in the video that closing a deal with a bank always feels exciting, and it looks great on paper, which signals validation. However, behind the scenes, it’s a different story, and banks are extremely slow to move, cautious, conservative, and are bound by layers of internal processes.

Furthermore, Schwartz stated that the team focused a lot on banks, because landing a press release with a major institution looked like progress. It felt like a milestone, and over time, the firm realized most of them were only in it for the optics.

data.description:

THE UNITED STATES

Vancouver, British Columbia--(Newsfile Corp. - August 1, 2025) - Quimbaya Gold Inc. (OTCQB: QIMGF) (FSE: K05) ("Quimbaya" or the "Company") announces that Denarius Metals Corp. has elected to terminate the binding Letter of Intent (the "LOI") previously announced on May 7, 2025. The LOI contemplated the formation of a 50:50 joint venture to advance the formalization of artisanal mining at Quimbaya's Tahami Project in the Segovia District of Colombia.

Quimbaya thanks Denarius for the time and consideration given to this opportunity. While the parties were unable to reach a definitive agreement, the Company appreciates the constructive dialogue and shared interest in advancing responsible development in one of Colombia's most prolific gold regions.

Quimbaya retains 100% ownership of the Tahami Project, including the drill-ready Tahami South. The Company remains focused on executing its fully funded 2025-2026 exploration program, which includes a 4,000-meter drill campaign scheduled to commence at Tahami South soon.

In parallel, Quimbaya will continue to pursue alternative structures to support the formalization of artisanal mining in the region, aligning with its long-standing commitment to responsible mining, inclusive economic participation, and strong community engagement.

"This is a strategically important district, and we remain confident in both the geological potential of Tahami and the strength of our position," said Alexandre P. Boivin, Chief Executive Officer. "Our exploration plans are on track, and we continue to evaluate opportunities that can responsibly advance the project and generate long-term value for all stakeholders."

About Quimbaya

Quimbaya aims to discover gold resources through exploration and acquisition of mining properties in the prolific mining districts of Colombia. Managed by an experienced team in the mining sector, Quimbaya is focused on three projects in the regions of Segovia (Tahami Project), Puerto Berrio (Berrio Project), and Abejorral (Maitamac Project), all located in Antioquia Province, Colombia.
